cells.label: genitive
cells.function: possession, absence, partitive; after many prepositions

cells.label: accusative
cells.function: direct object (= nom inanimate / = gen animate)

cells.label: prepositional
cells.function: location/topic; only after prepositions (в, на, о)

conditioning: FUSIONAL: a single ending fuses case × number × gender × declension class (3 main declensions); no clean one-suffix-per-case segmentation — the hard contrast with agglutinative Turkish

fit_none.note: because case fuses with gender/number/declension, the same surface ending realises different cases across classes (-а = gen.sg masc OR nom.sg fem), and the segmenter readily confuses noun endings with verb/adjective morphology — the expected residue for a fusional language

evidence_citations.stat: projected dep-role covariation over THOT alignment (nominative Ø→nsubj clean; obliques noisier)
endings recur but a single ending maps to multiple case×gender×number cells — low role purity

prose: rus has a six-term CASE system (nom/gen/dat/acc/instr/prep), but unlike Turkish it is FUSIONAL: one ending simultaneously encodes case, number, gender, and declension class, so there is no clean one-suffix-per-case signature to recover. Expect the data-driven detector to find the zero-marked nominative reliably and only fragments of the obliques (e.g. instrumental -ми/-ами), and to confuse noun endings with verb (-ть/-л/-ся) and adjective morphology. Recommendation: a fusional language needs a paradigm-cell (declension-table) approach, not the suffix-family detector that works for agglutinative case.
